claims.claim_text: A platform team at a payments company had a checkout incident with elevated public API latency, healthy-looking downstream services, broker writes still accepted, and customer checkout timeouts.
claims.anchor_text: A platform team at a payments company had a checkout incident that looked impossible from the dashboards. The public API showed elevated latency, the payment service looked healthy, the inventory service had no obvious errors, and the message broker was still accepting writes. Every team could prove its own service was mostly fine, yet customers were seeing timeouts after submitting orders. The hardest part was not fixing the bug; it was finding the exact place where the request stopped behaving like the system diagram promised.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In a checkout system, a latency incident can look impossible from dashboards: the public API shows elevated latency, individual downstream services look healthy, the broker still accepts writes, and customers still see timeouts after submitting orders. The hardest part is finding the exact place where the request stopped behaving like the system diagram promised.
claims.rationale: This is framed as a real incident but has no attribution, so the rewrite removes the unverifiable event framing while preserving the operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: A trace records one logical operation across a distributed system, and spans are timed units of work with parent-child relationships, attributes, status, and timing.
claims.rationale: The OpenTelemetry traces documentation directly defines traces, spans, span context, attributes, status, and parent-child relationships.

claims.claim_text: Trace ID, span ID, parent span ID, and context injection/extraction connect spans across service boundaries; missing propagation can produce separate traces.
claims.rationale: The OpenTelemetry traces documentation directly discusses span context, propagation, and how spans form a distributed trace.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry bootstrap installs detected instrumentation packages, and opentelemetry-instrument can run a Python service with configured exporters such as console export.
claims.rationale: The OpenTelemetry Python zero-code instrumentation docs cover bootstrap installation, opentelemetry-instrument, and exporter configuration.

claims.claim_text: The OpenTelemetry Collector is preferred in production because it centralizes retries, batching, redaction, sampling, and routing.
claims.rationale: The Collector documentation covers the receiver, processor, exporter pipeline and central processing of telemetry before export.

claims.claim_text: Tempo is optimized around cost-efficient trace storage, trace ID lookup, Grafana correlation, and TraceQL-supported search paths rather than heavy indexing of every span attribute.
claims.rationale: Grafana Tempo architecture documentation discusses its storage model, query path, TraceQL, and Grafana workflow integration.

claims.claim_text: Jaeger all-in-one stores data in memory by default, exposes UI/query/collector components, and can accept OTLP when enabled.
claims.rationale: The Jaeger repository README directly describes the all-in-one image and its in-memory storage and bundled components.

claims.claim_text: The Collector can receive OTLP from applications, batch spans, apply processors, export traces, redact attributes, add Kubernetes metadata, perform tail sampling, and route traces.
claims.rationale: The Collector docs cover receivers, processors, exporters, and collector deployment as a central telemetry processing point.

claims.claim_text: TraceQL is Tempo's query language for finding and analyzing traces by service, duration, status, and attributes.
claims.rationale: The Tempo architecture documentation covers TraceQL and Tempo query workflows.

claims.claim_text: The trace-volume example says full capture would be several TB per day and that 10 percent head sampling sharply reduces storage but can miss rare failures.
claims.anchor_text: At full capture, that is several TB each day.
claims.suggested_rewrite: At full capture, that can become a large daily storage load.
claims.rationale: The precise daily-volume statement is an illustrative calculation rather than a sourced statistic; the rewrite removes the specific quantity.

claims.claim_text: Head-based sampling decides at trace start; tail-based sampling waits until trace data arrives so the collector can keep traces based on errors, latency, routes, or tenants, at higher memory and operational cost.
claims.rationale: OpenTelemetry sampling documentation directly contrasts head and tail sampling and explains the buffering trade-off.

claims.claim_text: Sampling policies depend on correct span status and route attributes; missing error status or raw-ID route attributes can cause tail sampling to drop important traces or become noisy.
claims.rationale: OpenTelemetry sampling guidance supports preserving important traces through sampling policies and the need for usable span data.

claims.claim_text: Grafana exemplars can attach a trace ID to a metric observation and allow drill-down from metrics or logs to trace details.
claims.rationale: Grafana exemplar documentation defines exemplars as links between metric observations and traces and describes trace drill-down.

claims.claim_text: The sentence cites Loki docs for a claim that Loki, Elasticsearch, or another log backend can parse and filter trace_id and span_id fields.
claims.anchor_text: The fields should be plain strings that [Loki, Elasticsearch, or another log backend can parse and filter](https://grafana.com/docs/loki/latest/logql/).
claims.suggested_rewrite: The fields should be plain strings that your log backend can parse and filter.
claims.rationale: The linked Loki source supports Loki querying but does not directly support the named Elasticsearch capability, so the rewrite removes the off-source vendor claim.

claims.claim_text: Trace context can cross asynchronous boundaries when producers inject context into message headers and consumers extract it before starting work; missing this disconnects producer and consumer traces.
claims.rationale: OpenTelemetry tracing documentation covers context propagation across process boundaries and resulting trace continuity.

claims.claim_text: Secrets, tokens, full addresses, payment details, and sensitive personal data should not be put into span attributes; hashing or redaction should be considered for permitted identifiers.
claims.rationale: OWASP logging guidance directly warns against recording sensitive data and supports redaction/minimization principles for telemetry-like records.

claims.claim_text: service.name, service.version, deployment.environment, k8s.namespace.name, and cluster attributes are resource attributes used to identify and compare workloads and environments in traces.
claims.rationale: OpenTelemetry resource documentation defines resources and resource attributes used to identify telemetry-producing entities.

claims.claim_text: http.route route templates are better bounded attributes for aggregation than raw full paths such as http.target with unique IDs.
claims.rationale: OpenTelemetry HTTP semantic conventions document route-template attributes and HTTP span attributes.

claims.claim_text: The Jaeger HotROD demo is a traced demo application where one user action creates several spans.
claims.rationale: The Jaeger HotROD example repository is the primary source for the demo application used in the lab.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry: Traces
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary reference for traces, spans, span context, attributes, status, and request paths across services.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry: Sampling
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains head and tail sampling trade-offs and why sampling policy affects incident evidence.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Collector
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the Collector pipeline and the processors used for batching, sampling, routing, enrichment, and redaction.

further_reading.title: Grafana Tempo Architecture
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains Tempo’s storage, ingestion, query path, TraceQL, and Grafana correlation workflow.

notes: Existing pool sources cover OpenTelemetry traces, sampling, Collector, Tempo architecture, and Loki. Additional proposed sources cover W3C Trace Context, Jaeger all-in-one and HotROD, Grafana exemplars, OpenTelemetry Python zero-code instrumentation, OpenTelemetry resource and HTTP semantic conventions, and OWASP logging guidance.
